serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged portion is one can or pouch of approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing approximately 60 kcal and 14.3 g of carbohydrates including 14.3 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Snow Fungus with Wolfberry (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for snow fungus with wolfberry (less sugar, packaged), cr_id D05010035. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened herbal beverage with 5.72g sugar/100g
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged snow fungus wolfberry dessert soup. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous lightly sweetened herbal beverage entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The beverage contains 5.72g sugar/100g (predominantly sucrose and glucose from added sugar and wolfberry), negligible fibre (0.12g/100g), and no fat or protein to attenuate glycaemic response. GL per 250g serving = (55 × 14.3) / 100 = 7.9 (low-medium). This is a conservative estimate; actual GI may be slightly higher given the liquid matrix and rapid gastric emptying.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.72g/100g) in a liquid beverage exceeds the 5g/100g beverage sugar threshold; low sodium (5mg/100g) is favourable but does not offset the sugar-driven blood pressure risk pathway.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this packaged sweetened beverage; the 5.72g sugar/100g in liquid form independently raises blood pressure risk via the AHA/WHO sugar-driven pathway, and the 'less sugar' label does not bring it below the clinical threshold — choose the unsweetened version or prepare fresh snow fungus soup at home without added sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.72g sugar/100g and negligible dietary fibre (0.12g/100g); liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes — the 5.72g sugar/100g in liquid form is rapidly absorbed without fibre buffering, triggering the SSB diabetes gate; the 'less sugar' designation does not make it clinically safe, and unsweetened home-prepared snow fungus soup is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar (5.72g/100g) in a ready-to-drink beverage contributes to excess free sugar intake linked to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk; fat and sodium are negligible, which is favourable.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should treat this as a sugar-sweetened beverage and limit consumption — while fat (0g) and sodium (5mg/100g) are negligible, the 5.72g sugar/100g in liquid form contributes to free sugar load and associated cardiovascular risk per WHO 2015 guidelines; opt for unsweetened snow fungus preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (5.72g/100g) with negligible fibre negates the potential anti-inflammatory benefits of snow fungus beta-glucans and wolfberry antioxidants in this packaged form.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Despite the anti-inflammatory potential of snow fungus (beta-glucans) and wolfberry (zeaxanthin, polysaccharides), the added sugar in this packaged beverage is pro-inflammatory and makes it unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis management — prepare fresh unsweetened snow fungus soup at home to retain the beneficial compounds without the inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Snow Fungus with Wolfberry Dessert Soup (Less Sugar, Packaged) is a ready-to-drink sweet herbal dessert beverage popular in Singapore and among Chinese communities across Southeast Asia. It is made from Tremella fuciformis (snow fungus, also called silver ear fungus), wolfberry (Lycium barbarum, also known as goji berry), water, and a reduced quantity of added sugar. The beverage is commercially packaged — typically in cans or pouches of approximately 250 ml — and is heat-processed for shelf stability. The texture is lightly gelatinous due to the natural polysaccharides released by snow fungus during cooking, and the flavour is mildly sweet with a subtle earthy-floral note from the wolfberry. Nutritionally, this product is very low in energy (24 kcal/100g), contains no protein or fat, and derives virtually all its calories from carbohydrates (5.92g/100g), of which 5.72g/100g is sugar. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.12g/100g and sodium is very low at 5mg/100g. Despite the 'less sugar' label, the sugar content (5.72g/100g) exceeds the 5g/100g beverage threshold used in clinical dietary guidelines for diabetes and hypertension risk assessment. Snow fungus provides beta-glucan polysaccharides with potential immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory properties, and wolfberry contributes zeaxanthin and polysaccharides with antioxidant activity; however, these benefits are attenuated in the packaged sweetened form. This beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) for clinical purposes and is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and those following halal or gluten-free diets. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low) based on analogous lightly sweetened herbal beverages, with a glycemic load of approximately 7.9 per 250g serving.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010035) with confidence 0.95; this is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. The food identity, macros, and secondary nutrients are all taken from this exact record. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence) as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this packaged herbal beverage contains no protein-contributing ingredients in meaningful quantities; snow fungus and wolfberry at the concentrations used in this product contribute negligible protein. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this product contains no fat-contributing ingredients; it is a water-based herbal beverage with no added oils or dairy. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source and are treated as confirmed zero or below detection threshold for this product formulation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid panel data for this specific packaged snow fungus wolfberry beverage are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on lightly sweetened herbal beverage entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product has been published. The 'less sugar' designation reduces sugar relative to the standard version but does not bring the product below clinical SSB thresholds (5g sugar/100g for beverages). Ontology codes are approximate; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 term exists for packaged snow fungus wolfberry dessert soup.
